text: The surgeon performs a wide Cocker maneuver, suspending the duodenum from four corners. Using laparoscopic ultrasound, the ampulla is localized. A longitudinal duodenotomy is created, with two stitches placed superiorly and inferiorly to the ampulla for mobilization and retraction during dissection. Circumferential dissection is carried down to the pancreas using monopolar scissors.

The surgeon identifies the biliary and pancreatic ducts and transects them using scissors without energy application. The common bile duct is then spatulated with POTS scissors, followed by the creation of a choledochoduodenostomy using interrupted V-O vial stitches.

The surgeon secures the common bile duct to the pancreatic duct, forming a new septum. A running IVO bicryl stitch is used to take full-thickness bites of the pancreas and duodenum, repairing the duodenal defect. The stitch is continued inferiorly to incorporate the duodenal mucosa.

The surgeon begins a second running stitch on the contralateral side of the duodenum, running it inferiorly to meet the existing stitch. Once the two sutures meet, they are tied together, completing the reconstruction of the posterior wall of the duodenum.

The surgeon performs a two-layer closure of the duodenal anatomy. The first layer is closed using a 4-0 running bicurl suture, followed by the second layer closed with a 3-0 bicurl Connell stitch, resulting in the final appearance of the closed duodenal anatomy.
The surgeon inspects the suture line along the dual anatomy and places a drain. The surgical field shows no immediate complications.
